description: The classic Nigerian breakfast and quick meal — boiled yam with a rich, savoury tomato and egg sauce cooked in onions, pepper, and seasoning. Simple, fast, and deeply satisfying when done right.

ingredients.name: white yam (puna yam)
ingredients.note: Puna yam is the standard for boiling. Avoid water yam — it is too watery and sticky for this. Peel thickly — the outer layer just under the skin is often fibrous.

ingredients.name: eggs
ingredients.note: Room temperature eggs cook more evenly. Do not beat until just before adding to the pan.

ingredients.name: plum tomatoes
ingredients.note: Blend for a smoother sauce or dice finely for a chunkier texture. Both are valid — personal preference.

ingredients.name: red bell pepper (tatashe)
ingredients.note: Adds sweetness and colour to the sauce. Optional but recommended.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: 1 gives background heat without being aggressive. Skip for a completely mild sauce.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 into the tomato blend, 1 sliced for frying in the sauce. The fried onion gives the sauce its base sweetness.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: For frying the sauce base. Do not use too much — the sauce should not be oily.

ingredients.name: seasoning cube
ingredients.note: Crush before adding to the sauce.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season the yam water and the egg sauce separately.

ingredients.name: spring onions (scallions)
ingredients.note: Slice and add at the end for freshness and colour.

ingredients.name: curry powder
ingredients.note: A tiny amount adds warmth and colour to the egg sauce. Optional.

ingredients.name: water
ingredients.note: For boiling the yam. Enough to fully submerge the yam pieces.

steps.title: Peel and Boil the Yam
steps.instruction: Use a sharp knife to peel the yam, cutting generously — peel off the outer skin and the thin layer immediately beneath it, which is often slightly fibrous and bitter. The flesh underneath should be completely white and smooth. Slice into rounds or wedges about 3–4cm thick — uniform thickness ensures even cooking. Rinse the cut pieces under cold water to remove the surface starch. Place in a pot, cover generously with cold water, add a good pinch of salt, and bring to a boil over high heat. Reduce to medium and cook until a fork passes through the thickest piece with zero resistance.
micro_steps: Place the yam tuber on a stable surface and cut into manageable sections
Peel each section with a sharp knife — cut generously to remove both the skin and the fibrous layer beneath
The exposed flesh should be completely white and smooth
Slice into rounds or wedges approximately 3–4cm thick — keep thickness consistent
Rinse all pieces under cold running water
Place in a pot and cover with cold water — enough to fully submerge all pieces
Add a generous pinch of salt to the water
Bring to a full boil over high heat
Reduce to medium and cook for 20–25 minutes
At 20 minutes, pierce the thickest piece with a fork — it should glide through with no resistance
If any resistance remains, continue cooking and test every 5 minutes
prep_notes: Put the yam on to boil first — it takes the longest and the sauce comes together quickly
Blend or dice the tomatoes, tatashe, and scotch bonnet while the yam boils
Take the eggs out of the fridge now if they were cold — room temperature eggs cook more evenly
Do not beat the eggs until you are standing at the stove ready to add them
steps.sensory_cue: Properly boiled yam is creamy white, slightly fluffy at the cut edges, and smells faintly earthy and sweet. A fork inserted into the thickest piece should slide in and out with absolutely zero resistance — like pressing into soft butter. The cooking water will look slightly starchy and cloudy when the yam is ready.
steps.failure_risk: Undercooked yam is starchy, waxy, and unpleasant to eat — it tastes raw and feels gluey. Always test the thickest piece and do not drain until it is completely done. Do not rush — adding the yam to the plate still slightly undercooked is the most common yam mistake.

steps.title: Blend the Pepper Base
steps.instruction: While the yam boils, prepare the sauce components. Place the plum tomatoes, half the onion, the tatashe if using, and the scotch bonnet into a blender. Do not add water. Blend until completely smooth. If you prefer a chunkier sauce with visible tomato pieces, dice the tomatoes finely instead and skip the blender entirely. Slice the remaining half onion into thin rings or half-moons and set aside.
micro_steps: Roughly chop the plum tomatoes and add to the blender
Add half the onion, roughly chopped
Add the tatashe if using, roughly chopped
Add the scotch bonnet
Do not add water
Blend until completely smooth — about 20 seconds
Pour into a bowl and set aside
Slice the remaining onion half into thin rings or half-moons — set aside
steps.sensory_cue: The blended base should be smooth, thick, and a vibrant red-orange colour. It should smell fresh and sharp — the raw tomato and pepper scent is strong at this stage. If it looks very thin and watery, the tomatoes had high water content — that is fine, just plan to fry the base slightly longer.
steps.failure_risk: Adding water to the blend produces a thin sauce that takes longer to fry down and produces a watery egg sauce. Blend without water and let the natural moisture of the tomatoes do the work.

steps.title: Fry the Sauce Base
steps.instruction: Heat the vegetable oil in a wide pan over medium-high heat. Add the sliced onion and fry, stirring occasionally, for 3 minutes until soft and starting to turn golden at the edges. Pour in the blended tomato and pepper mixture — it will sizzle. Fry the base, stirring every 90 seconds, for 8–10 minutes until the liquid reduces significantly, the colour deepens, and the oil floats visibly to the surface. Add the curry powder if using and stir in. The raw tomato smell must be completely gone before the eggs go in.
micro_steps: Heat 3 tablespoons of vegetable oil in a wide pan over medium-high heat
Add the sliced onion and fry for 3 minutes until soft and lightly golden
Pour in the blended tomato and pepper mixture
Stir immediately to combine with the onion
Fry over medium-high heat, stirring every 90 seconds, for 8–10 minutes
The base will visibly reduce and darken as it fries
At the 8-minute mark, look for oil floating clearly on the surface — this means it is ready
Add ¼ tsp curry powder if using and stir in
Confirm the raw tomato smell is completely gone — replaced by a rich, cooked aroma
steps.sensory_cue: The fully fried tomato base should look visibly reduced and darker than the raw blend — a deeper, richer red-orange. The oil should be floating clearly on the surface. The sharp, raw tomato smell should have transformed into a rich, cooked, slightly sweet aroma. If any sourness or rawness remains, the base needs more time.
steps.failure_risk: Adding eggs to an underfried tomato base produces a sour, raw-tasting egg sauce — the raw tomato flavour clings to the eggs and cannot be cooked out once they are scrambled in. The base must be fully fried and the raw smell completely gone before the eggs go in. The oil rising to the surface is the confirmation.

steps.title: Add Seasoning
steps.instruction: Reduce the heat to medium. Crush the seasoning cube to powder and add it to the fried tomato base. Add salt. Stir well and taste — the sauce should be well-seasoned and slightly bold at this point, because the eggs will absorb some of the seasoning and mellow the overall flavour when added.
micro_steps: Reduce heat to medium
Crush the seasoning cube to powder and add to the base
Stir until fully dissolved
Add a pinch of salt
Stir and taste — it should be bold and well-seasoned
Adjust with a little more salt if it tastes flat
The base should taste slightly more intense than you want the finished sauce
steps.sensory_cue: The seasoned base should taste bold, savoury, and slightly intense — perhaps slightly more seasoned than feels comfortable. This is correct because the eggs will absorb some of it and mellow it out. If the base already tastes perfect before the eggs, the finished sauce will taste slightly flat.
steps.failure_risk: Under-seasoning the base before the eggs go in produces a flat, bland egg sauce. The eggs absorb seasoning but cannot create it — bold base equals well-seasoned finished sauce. Season confidently at this stage.

steps.title: Add the Eggs
steps.instruction: Beat the 4 eggs in a bowl with a fork — just enough to break the yolks and combine them with the whites, not so much that they become frothy. Reduce the heat to medium-low. Pour the beaten eggs evenly over the tomato base in the pan. Leave undisturbed for about 30 seconds until the edges and bottom begin to set and turn opaque. Then stir gently with a spatula using slow, wide folding strokes — you want large, soft, custardy curds of egg distributed through the tomato sauce. Stop the moment the eggs look just set but still have a slight glossy softness — they will finish cooking from the residual heat of the pan.
micro_steps: Beat 4 eggs in a bowl with a fork — just break and combine, do not over-beat
Reduce pan heat to medium-low
Pour the beaten eggs evenly over the entire surface of the tomato base
Leave undisturbed for 30 seconds — the bottom and edges will begin to set
Use a spatula to fold gently in slow, wide strokes — 3 or 4 strokes maximum
Aim for large, soft custardy curds — not fine scrambled egg
Stop folding the moment the eggs look just set with a slight glossy softness
Remove from heat immediately — do not wait for them to look fully dry
steps.sensory_cue: The eggs should look soft, custardy, and deeply coloured from the tomato sauce running through them. They should be just set — not dry or rubbery, not runny. Think of it as eggs scrambled inside a tomato sauce rather than scrambled eggs with tomato added separately. The colour contrast of the golden egg curds against the red-orange tomato base is the visual mark of a well-made egg sauce.
steps.failure_risk: Overcooked eggs become dry, rubbery, and completely separate from the sauce — they turn into tough little pieces floating in tomato rather than soft curds of egg and sauce together. Remove from heat the moment they look just set with a glossy softness. High heat at this stage is the most common mistake — medium-low only, and off the heat before they look fully done.

steps.title: Drain Yam and Serve
steps.instruction: The moment the yam is done, drain it immediately into a colander — do not leave it sitting in the hot cooking water, as it will continue cooking and become waterlogged and fragile. Plate the yam pieces and spoon the egg sauce generously alongside or over them. Scatter sliced spring onions over the top if using. Serve immediately — yam and egg sauce is best eaten fresh and does not hold well.
micro_steps: Test the yam one final time — fork should meet zero resistance in the thickest piece
Drain immediately into a colander the moment it is done
Do not leave in the hot water for any reason
Arrange yam pieces on serving plates
Spoon the egg sauce generously alongside or over the yam
Scatter sliced spring onions over the top if using
Serve immediately
steps.sensory_cue: The plate should show a beautiful contrast — white, fluffy, steaming yam pieces against the bright orange-red egg sauce with soft, golden egg curds distributed throughout. When you break a piece of yam, it should steam and look creamy white inside. One mouthful of yam with egg sauce together should be creamy, savoury, and immediately satisfying.
steps.failure_risk: Yam left sitting in the hot cooking water after it is done continues cooking rapidly and becomes waterlogged — it breaks apart on the plate and the texture turns from fluffy and firm to mushy and wet. Drain the moment it passes the fork test. Also, covering the egg sauce after cooking traps steam and makes the eggs watery — leave it uncovered until served.
